mnemonics.title: Pigeonhole Recall
mnemonics.content: PIGEON: P-I-G-E-O-N stands for 'If n items > m holes, Then at least one hole has >1 item' - Remember the picture of pigeons trying to fit into limited holes!

mnemonics.title: Generalized Formula
mnemonics.content: CEILING is your friend: Remember 'C' for 'Ceiling' in ⌈n/m⌉ - the ceiling function always gives the minimum guaranteed maximum!

mnemonics.title: Application Types
mnemonics.content: HASH-BDAY-HAIR: Hash tables, Birthday paradox, Hair counts - three classic CS applications of pigeonhole principle

acronyms.expansion: Ramsey Number - smallest vertices to guarantee m-clique or n-independent set
acronyms.usage: Used in graph theory extensions of pigeonhole principle. R(3,3)=6.

acronyms.expansion: Complete graph with n vertices where every pair is connected
acronyms.usage: In Ramsey theory, edges of Kₙ are colored to find monochromatic subgraphs.

acronyms.expansion: Ceiling function - smallest integer greater than or equal to x
acronyms.usage: In generalized pigeonhole: minimum items in largest box = ⌈n/m⌉

visualTips.visualization: Draw 5 circles (holes) and try to place 6 dots (pigeons). Show one circle must have 2 dots.
Stack of shelves with items. Divide n items into m shelves. Show ceil(n/m) is always on some shelf.
Draw 365 calendar boxes. Show how 23 people create 253 pairs, exceeding half the boxes.
Draw hash table as row of mailboxes. Drop 101 letters into 100 slots - overflow guaranteed.

analogies.analogy: Like putting 10 cars in 9 parking spots - impossible without doubling up somewhere!
Distributing 100 cookies among 7 children - one child gets at least ⌈100/7⌉ = 15 cookies.
If 100 packets must pass through 50 routers, at least one router handles 2 packets (ceil(100/50)=2).
At a party, dividing n friends among 2 handshake types (know/don't know) - someone gets same number as someone else!
